blocks.text: If You Did Not Receive ALL Pages, Please Call Our Office
8402 West Centennial Parkway 8205 West Warm Springs Road 2930 West Horizon Ridge Parkway
ICD 10: S/P 1 ..TKA skilled nursing visit for
322 Karen Ave Unit 1203 Las Vegas, NV
Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield PPO/EPO
F/U Knee Arthritis, Primary, Left evaluated on September 30, 2025
HPI: This is a 71 year old male who:
1. is following up for Knee Arthritis, Primary on the left knee joint. He was seen on September
30, 2025, at which time he was treated with Viscosupplementation - Orthovisc.
The patient presents for status check.
Right Knee: skin intact, no rashes or lesions.
Left Knee: Old well healed open
Right Knee: mild knee effusion, medial joint
Left Knee: mild knee effusion, medial joint
Right Knee: Patellar Grind Test: positive
Left Knee: Patellar Grind Test: positive
Right patella tracking: normal and crepitus 1
Left patella tracking: normal and crepitus 1
Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with degenerative joint disease of the knee can achieve
some measure of pain relief from nonsurgical options like medications, lifestyle modification, exercise and physical
therapy, and use of supportive devices or change in footwear. The importance of maintaining an ideal body weight or
losing weight to reduce stress on the knee joint is a cornestone of nonoperative management.
NSAIDS : I discussed with the patient that NSAIDs should be taken with food. Prolonged use of NSAIDs can result in
the development of stomach ulcers or bleeding. Patient advised to stop taking NSAIDs if abdominal pain occurs. The
patient verbalized understanding of the proper use and possible adverse effects of NSAIDs. All of the patient's
NSAIDs: Discussed Meloxicam for pain. Take with food. If medication helps he will speak with his PCP about long
Active Therapy : Active therapies include stretching exercises, weight training (quadriceps strengthening), and
cardiovascular non-impact conditioning. Weight reduction may be emphasized as appropriate.
Passive Therapy : Passive therapies include ice, heat, massage, ultrasound, and electrical stimulation. Your therapist
will decide which of these interventions will be most beneficial to you.
Intraarticular steroid injection : I discussed with the patient that this involves the injection of a potent antiinflammatory
substance (cortisone) into the knee joint. Risks include whitening of the skin at the injection site and a transient rise in
blood glucose. Complications are extremely rare; infection is the most common. There is no rule as to how many
injections can be given. Because some research has shown that too much cortisone can damage cartilage, most
physicians limit the number of shots they will give you.
Intraarticular viscosupplementation : I discussed with the patient that this procedure involves the injection of artificial
joint fluid into the knee. The fluid acts as a lubricant, shock absorber, and an antiinflammatory agent. Depending on
the specific agent used you will receive one to five shots, each shot one week apart. This intervention is most effective
if used in early stage osteoarthritis. Side effects include knee swelling and pain at the injection site. Long term efficacy
is not currently known. Complications include but are not limited to infection and joint swelling.
Total knee replacement : I explained to the patient that this is an option for patients that have severe pain in their knee
from arthritis who have failed conservative management. The diseased parts of the knee are replaced with metal and
plastic. Approximately 90% of patients who have total knee replacement experience a significant reduction in pain and
a dramatic improvement in their ability to perform common activities of daily living. High impact activities should be
avoided after surgery, if possible, to prolong the life of the implant. Serious complications occur in less than 2% of
patients. Risks include but are not limited to infection, injury to blood vessels and nerves, blood clots, heart
complications, lung complications, stroke, and stiffness.
I reviewed and discussed the results of the X-Ray(s) with the patient.
After counseling the patient, we decided on the following plan for the LEFT KNEE: Total knee replacement

I reviewed and discussed the results of the X-Ray(s) with the patient.
Follow up PRN for: Further Evaluation and Management - (with Physician). Other Instructions:
will schedule for pre-op appt once medically cleared, with DR. Nevins
Patient presents for bilateral knee follow up. He has end stage arthritis of bilateral knees. He would like to proceed with
I discussed with him that given his current minimal pain, the goal of the surgery should be to give him more functionality in
his knees. I explained to him that there are no guarantees with total knee replacement, and that he may have more pain
following knee replacement which he fully understands. His biggest complaint with his knees is that during periods of
standing that it becomes difficulty to move again and issues going up and down stairs.
Understanding the risks, we will proceed with total knee replacement. He will follow up with Dr. Nevins for pre-operative
Electronically Signed By: Russell T Nevins, MD, 03/18/2026 06:39 AM PDT
I saw the patient with Viet Nguyen, elicited a history, performed an exam, and developed an impression and plan. I was present for all key aspects of the
visit. My impression is as follows:
Electronically Signed By: Viet A Nguyen, PA-C, 03/17/2026 08:01 AM PDT
